## 4. 15 Books That Helped Make Me My First Million

I've read hundreds of books on making
money, but these 15 are the ones I keep
coming back to. Not theory, not hype,
books that actually changed how I think,
how I build, and how I make money so
that you can catch something over and
over again. The best business books are
not the ones you read once. They're the
ones that you can't get out of your
head. So, these are my most important
books. This isn't a listical, no top 10
filler. It's what I've actually been
studying. And these tools now are going
to go from me and how I made money to
you. First book is wild. This is called
Only the Paranoid Survive. I love this.
This is from our billionaires and
builders section. This is by Andy Grove
who was famously the chairman of the
board of Intel. Andy was like one of the
greats. He turned Intel, which was a
hardware company, into a global
powerhouse. He escaped Hungary during
the revolution. He earned a PhD and
became Intel's CEO, searing the company
through like literally one of the worst
parts in business history, from memory
chips to microprocessors. But what
you're learning from this is the
playbook for how to move fast and stay
alert even when everything is falling
apart. His entire philosophy is
basically summed up in one line, which
is success breeds complacency.
Complacency breeds failure. Only the
paranoid survive. But when he says
paranoid, he doesn't mean fearful. He
means vigilant. So staying awake when
everybody else is asleep, watching for
those small signs that the world is
shifting underneath you because business
and your career, they're not going to
die from a bad quarter. They die from
missing the moment when the game itself
changes. So Grove breaks down this idea
of what to do when change happens into a
few frameworks. First is the six forces
of competition. And it's a twist on
Michael Porter's five forces, but Grove
adds one more. Complimenters. Companies
whose products make yours stronger. You
can see all six here. competitors,
customers, suppliers, substitutes,
potential competitors, and complimenters
and look for anything shifting 10x.
That's how you spot inflection points
before they kill you. Then there's what
he calls the adaptive response
framework, or as he puts it, let chaos
rain, then rain it in. So phase one is
open the floor. So encourage ideas,
disscent, uncomfortable truths. Let
people in the edges of your business
tell you what they're seeing because
change always shows up at the edges
first. Then phase two, once the
direction is clear, tighten the focus.
Align resources, commit fully, move
fast. That's how you balance exploration
and execution. What's powerful about
Grove is that he doesn't just talk
theory and lived this with billions on
the line. And he says most companies
don't die because they make the wrong
move. They die because they don't commit
to the right one fast enough. They
freeze. This is not about tech. If
you're not in tech, it doesn't matter.
It's about survival. And I think this is
one to read, not listen to. It's kind of
dense. It's framework heavy, but it has
a ton of very specific things you can do
to change your life. And I think if you
do it right, you'll find what he calls
signal to noise, which is how do you
rewire your brain to listen to the
things that are actually going on? How
do we make sure we're not complacent?
How do we make sure that we continue to
grow? Because we're not trying to sound
smart. We're trying to win. And if you
read these couple of books, I think you
can be a winner, too. Next, we've got
How to Make a Few Billion Dollars by
Brad Jacobs. Brad I'm dying to have on
the podcast. So, maybe you guys can tag
him in the comments and tell him to get
on here. But he built eight companies,
six of them publicly traded. And these
aren't like Silicon Valley sexy

businesses. He literally started in
trash. His first big win was United
Waste Systems where he rolled up small
rural trash hauling companies and sold
the whole thing to Waste Management.
That was actually fascinating cuz at
some point they almost went bankrupt and
he bought it back for pennies on the
dollar. Then he built United Rentals

I'm obsessed with. He's got this one
line that I love in his strategy. I love
working with outrageously talented
people to deliver outsized returns.
Every time you get an opportunity to
work with people smarter than you, do it
because that is how you make money. He
talks about how to build a business that
runs his itself. The 108010 framework,
every business has a top 10% what only
you, the founder, the leader should do,
which is like strategy, vision, business
model, partnerships, high leverage. A
middle 80% which is what your team
should do. That's like clear plans,
delegated ownership, execution. Bottom
10%, nobody should really do this. You
should either eliminate it, outsource
it, or automate it. So this is like low
value, really redundant tasks. He also
talks about the Midas formula, which is
like how he's always able to raise
money. He's had to raise billions of
dollars. And the way that you do that is
you make sure that every single business
you have is profitable, growing at least
5% per year. You have a track record of

steps continuously over time will lead
to you making more money. He talks about
how most companies die because you don't
do the thing that is working to the max.
He's like, when you find something that
scales, exploit it rapidly. Otherwise,
you're giving your competitors time to

Jacobs does, he calls this a siege
mentality. So, when you find a moment of

he founded this group and made billions
not by following the crowd but by

is scared. He says, "Start by being
audacious." So the guy when he's a

"We're students. We know what students

[ __ ] idea how to manage apartments,
but he just asked. They put together a
little pitch, a little brochure, and
guess what? Amazingly, the landlord
bought our act. We sold a bunch of the
units for them and made a bunch of

built his fortune by doing what other
people wouldn't. He said, "Risk is the
ultimate differentiator. I've always had
a deep and complex relationship with
it." To me, risk-taking rests on the
ability to see all the variables and

just being aware. His entire framework
boils down to really three things.
First, spotting opportunities as a
contrarian. He looked where no one else
was looking. When markets froze, he
started buying. When people said it's
over, he saw a discount. and he believed
that fortunes were made in neglected
corners of the market. Sometimes that
might have been real estate, sometimes
media. If it's something out of favor
but fundamentally sound, that's your
signal. Second, liquidity and downside
first. He said, "I never worry about the
upside. I just make sure I can survive
the downside." And that's how to play
the game. You try to protect your bottom
line, your base. When everyone else was
chasing returns and being greedy, he was
calm and he was looking for the moment
where the market started to falter. And
maybe that's why he lasted for decades.
Third, simplicity and speed in
execution. He believed complexity kills
deals. So do I. So when everyone else
got all tangled up in spreadsheets and
analysis, he focused on what he actually
thought mattered. One or two variables
that would make the whole thing. This
book is packed with stories about how
you can do this exact same thing. If you
work in business, production, or any
highstakes environment, it'll make you

opportunity and handle risk. He said,
"Those who move fast usually win the
whole game." So move fast to read this
one. All right, section two is all about

you're not going to get very far. So
these next books are about leading other

One of the first ones is from Jaco

Number four, keep it simple. Complicated

What I love about this mindset is it

instead of saying how will I do this,

[ __ ] you level rich. One of the great

## 14. 9 Income Streams You Can Build While STILL an Employee

If I wanted to be wealthy, have less
risk, and have a higher likelihood of
figuring out where I could make the most
money, I need multiple income streams.
And in this video, I want to share with
you the nine income streams I grew while
having a job before I got known on the
internet. I wish somebody would have
told me this stuff. When I was working,
nobody talked about where they put their
money, why, and how much money they
actually made. In fact, they hid it.
Totally frowned upon to share any of
this. Depending on your situation in
life, the best side hustle or income
stream for you to pick is going to be
different. The most important thing to
understand when looking for more income
streams is that you want to find
leverage, a way of working smarter, not
harder, to achieve the same goal with
less effort. Consider it like using a
lever to open a bottle cap. You can
either attempt to manually brute force
it, which is time for money, or you can
use the right leverage for your
situation, and the leverage doesn't work
for you. So, how do you figure out which
income streams you have the right lever
for? There are four money levers at
different points you can use to build up
your income streams like I did. Let's
jump into what those are and I'll give
you all the exact things that I did.
First lever is time. Obviously, time is
all you got in the beginning early on.
Anytime you can trade your time for
somebody else's money, that's going to
be your superpower. Here is one of the
first income streams that I had, a
service business. I started a service
business that helped people do business
in Latin America. It was called Selling
South and basically did it on the side
with a colleague who worked with me at
the time. All we did was we helped
companies that wanted to do what we did
in Latin America by giving them
introductions. So, this company got paid
by the hour to create market entry to
those who also wanted to sell
investments into LAM. It wasn't a huge
income stream, but our first year, we
each took home about $40,000 a year. It
also led to a big job years later for
me, helping this woman, Nelly Galon, do
a part of her tour in the US for Latinos
and finance. And I never would have
gotten that opportunity without a couple
of long hours and nights doing the
service business. That's how I met
Gloria Stefan. That's how I got on
People in Espanol's red carpet. I think
I made 15K for that side gig with Nelly
Legalon, but I would have paid to
participate in all of that. Now, today,
I own another online service business.
This one's called Viral Cuts, and it's
made me realize this is something I wish
I had done when I was in my 20s. I
didn't have Selling South until I was
well into my 30s. It's so easy today to
start offering a service online. We've
paid contractors at Contrarian Thinking
5K for 10 to 20 hours a month doing
research, writing, design, short form
editing like this, finding an outsource
expert on sites like Upwork or Fiverr
for look at this like 5, 10, 20 bucks an
hour and then you charge $100, $200 an
hour. You can also start a barebones
sort of boring service business out in
the real world. You could do it for 500
bucks like we did with this pressure
washing company, window cleaning,
landscaping, etc. I didn't do that early
on. I did it with my consulting hours to
make intros. Here's what no one will
tell you about this, though. Service
businesses are a great way to start, but
I don't think they're a great way to
end. If you have custom services and
provide services before you get paid,
that means you're chasing invoices.
Scope creep is a real thing, which means
somebody asks you for this and you
instead deliver all of this, but you
don't get paid for this in the middle.
So, there's a reason there are so many
of them and very few of these service
businesses get huge. You want to have
somebody pay you before you offer a
service. You want to have somebody have
very specific things you deliver every
single time. You want to get paid for
every single hour of your time. And with
service businesses, that's hard to do in
the beginning. The last thing that's
hard for me with the service business, I
couldn't estimate very well what my
total time needed was. And that's
something you're going to want to do. So
don't start with a ton of these. Start
small if you're going to do a
service-based business. Number two, 9
to5. Hard truth. Your first 100K will
most likely come from your earned wages,
your income. For most people, it'll be
your first million. It was for me. I
made 37Kish when I got out of college.
And at the end of my time being employed
by someone else in finance, I made seven
figures as a private equity partner. It
was broken down like this. 20% salary.
So, I get this no matter what for my
day-to-day unless I get fired. 30%
carry, which is distributions for
performance. I'd get paid out a
percentage of the profits we made in our
investments. So, if we have a company
pay out, I don't know, $100,000 dividend
between us partners, I might get 8% of
that because that was the percentage
that I owned of the company. Then
another 35 to 40% was bonuses. These are
milestones hit. So, I'd get paid when we
hit our milestones, like a number of
deals done or a return profile hit. And
then maybe another 10 to 15% for
commissions in money raised. So you will
ideally have a gradually declining line
that starts at 9 to5 at the height and
eventually whittleles down to the least
portion. Why? Because earned income
working versus investment income or what
is deemed by the IRS as non-active
income is taxed at a startlingly lower
rate. So you keep more of what you make.

$622,000
by having a job, you pay 37%. If you use

double paying for the right to trade

was also highly skewed by my salary. And
I'd say probably accounted for 80 to 90%

## 18. A Once in a Lifetime Economic Reset is Coming…

I read something the other day that
stopped me cold. You have 3 minutes to
escape the underclass before AI takes
your job. I don't think you'll
be doomed by the time this video ends.
However, if you don't change in the next
3 years, you should be really worried.
We don't have decades before AI actually
reshapes the entire economy so violently
that most people become irrelevant by
default. Now, this video isn't for
people hoping things will go back to
normal because it won't. We are
heading into a future where labor
doesn't matter, where effort does not
earn you security. And if you don't own
anything in that system, you will not
just be underpaid, you'll be
powerless. But I do believe that there
is a way out, and we've researched it
for you today in this video. We're going
to show you how to escape the system
before it decides your value for you by
having an actual stake. But you
have to move now. We are on a path into
a post-labor economy where most people
won't be needed. And a small group of
people will be powered by AI and
machines. It's the logical endgame of
trends we've seen building for more than
a century. And if you don't pay
attention to it, the billionaires will
own you and us all. And you can see
here, the billionaires are already
putting their capital into this exact
play. They just don't want to
tell you about it. They want to say not
all jobs will be taken while they put
billions in and lay us off left and
right. So, you quite simply need to have
a real stake in whatever comes next.
It's why I'm all my richest friends
literally cannot sleep right now. You
know, they're calling me up, names you
know on the internet, talking about the
opportunity at their fingertips and how
they need to seize it and if they don't
understand AI, even they, who
are worth millions, are going to be out
of money. And so, let's talk about this
in a few really important ways. First, I
want to talk about compute, which is the
fuel behind the AI explosion. So, a lot
of people are thinking, why does it
suddenly feel like AI everywhere? Like,
where has this come from? And they're
right. In short, it's because the
computing power behind it has grown like
crazy in recent years. This is
really important since deep learning was
created in 2010,
>>
>> training computation has doubled every 6
months. From 2012 to 2018, OpenAI found
that the compute used in the largest
training programs grew by 300,000
times, basically doubling every 3.4
months. So, to put that in perspective,
Google's Gemini class systems now uses
around 100 million times more compute
than AlexNet did a decade ago. That's
like going from a bicycle to a rocket

Well, more compute doesn't guarantee

and me. I think we're trending toward to

by maybe next year or something, I mean,

century. Many think that we could

actually economically necessary
as producers. We're all optional. Since

have a post-consumer market because
fewer people have jobs, so fewer people

as much as 267%

## 21. Asking A Billionaire How To Make Money

i have a friend who's 40 and damn near a
billionaire one night i was asking
candidly how'd you make your fortune and
i was looking for things to replicate
and the simplicity of his response
surprised me he said i train my brain to
perform one task incredibly well one
task alone to never conform
that's it and i said okay that sounds
good but how do you do that can you
explain to me and we came to this what
i'll call the funnel to non-conformity
become a funnel for ideas let ideas
fight based on merit not emotion find
courage to act in non-conformity
and you'll have opportunities others
never see or are too scared to act on
then he gave me a series of ideas ways
that you could put this into work in
your life every single day and because i
don't have access to that many
billionaires i thought i'd share them
with you because they changed my life
and maybe they can change yours so the
first thing he said is don't believe in
anger anger is useful in physical fights
but it's a killer of logic it's a trick
of the mind pushing you into fight or
flight let me tell you what you can't do
well when you're fighting or flighting
think remove your anger
and you'll find clarity the second thing
was that controversy and conformity
are a conundrum and the conundrum is
that there's a spectrum where
controversies and conformity sit on two
ends of the line more conformity equals
less controversy and yet most of the
wins in life land on the edge of
controversy so choose your controversy
wisely but choose it the third was
reading as a diet he said be careful
what you read because it becomes the way
that you think number four was ideas
strongly stated and loosely held having
a strong opinion he said is the mark of
intellect but having to change it is the
mark of true intelligence only
politicians hold one stance forever for
fear of flip-flopping we mortals do
better to constantly experiment then the
fifth thing you told me was share your
mvps your minimal viable products every
time you produce a product or an idea
more come back tenfold so don't make it
perfect make it done
then let the market decide most people i
think wait for perfection of them in
everything that they do and he said most
the reason that he had created wealth is
because he knew that perfectionism would
never come so he shipped it builders
build number six is that warren buffett
doesn't optimize my friend actually met
with warren buffett sat down and had
dinner and a couple things stood out to
him but one thing in particular is that
people these days say things like well i
wake up at 5 00 a.m meditate work out
drink 72 ounces of chlorine water then i
stand up outside
you know then i go do a headstand
then i drink green juice okay
i guess you could go do those things or
you could do none of them and instead
focus on thinking clearly intently and
strategically before you pull any
triggers make it the one thing you need
to focus on each day
and that might be any morning routine
that some guru tries to sell you number
seven is boredom as a superpower the
best ideas need breathing room he said
they're a flower stretching for the sun
when you clutter them with meetings
you'll watch them die instead clear your
schedule every morning with no calls
prior to 10 am i do the same now paired
with two no-call days a week i need to
let my ideas breathe otherwise they die

We have a small business that we own and
this small business used Stripe, totally

uh this small business owner missed a

that they didn't want this product and

They missed like three over, let's call

"What happened?" They run an analysis

The wild part about this story is this

But the part that Stripe isn't thinking

audience, but do it anyway. Except don't
do something you hate forever. That was
a little moment in history where my life
changed drastically. From that moment
on, I said I never wanted to have to be
on Red Eyes to run a business anymore.
And I never wanted to have to run
somebody else's business for them. I
wanted to run my own business. At some
point, you're going to realize that you
don't like running the job or the
business that you're in. It'll just
happen eventually. It's happened to
almost everybody that I know. And you
have to know what your plan for exit is.
And you have to come into a business
with the knowledge that eventually you
might want to get divorced. And so, how
could you start with the end in mind?
Thankfully in this business I could talk
to the CEO of the company and him and I
were friends and I basically started
having a conversation about how I wanted
to exit, who could be my predecessor
that would come after me and what I
wanted to do next and maybe he would
even invest in my next venture. So if
you are going into something, you go in
with rosecolored glasses, really
excited, thinking everything's going to
win. But eventually what a win looks
like for you when you start is very
different than what a win looks like for

idea of redeyed flights to foreign lands
to hang out in Santiago to drink some
wine and hang out with a bunch of
Chileans sounded amazing. And by the end
of 5 years, 6 years, it wasn't so
amazing anymore. Assume that the person
that you're going to be tomorrow is not
the person that you are today and that
that other person is going to want
something bigger by that day. You need
to plan with the end in mind. The lesson
is you need to quit sometimes to find
your real yes. We got two more stories
for you, but I want to remind you
something. This isn't like failure porn

you see these things coming, you can one

And either of those is better than

the next story. The day that you realize
you actually hate your business. I love
this quote, but it also makes me hurt.
If your business depends on you, you
don't have a business, you have a job,

because you're working for a lunatic. A
friend of mine, Andrew Wilkinson, wrote

I wasn't having fun anymore. I liked
running 10erson companies. Now I had a
100 employees. I was the wrong person.
So, I did the only thing that made
sense. I fired myself. He made some

a psychopath that lied about their
entire resume. But in the 10 years
since, he's learned how to hire

to keep working for a psychopath
himself. A job and a business aren't

either of those things happened is
actually you or me. It's because you
didn't actually know what you wanted and
so you jumped into something. You
thought that it would fix that hole

or why people close up shop and never

doing, I don't know, $100 million a year

himself. Your job, if you're an

that you could move on to a bigger
position. If you own a business, it's to

almost everybody will eventually hate
their job. It's why most people don't
keep the same job or business for 60
years. If you expect that, then you
plan, then you exit. I'll tell you a
funny sort of embarrassing story. I um
did a little like a little medicine
journey, as the kids say it in in Austin
here. Basically during it, I realized
that I was stuck in a PowerPoint that
was representative of one of the
businesses that I run. For like 8 hours,
I was inside of this monotonous, awful
PowerPoint that made me want to gouge my
eyes out. There was like no joy, no
happiness in the world. And apparently,
because I'm such a business psychopath,
I didn't realize that, you know, I
already ran these three businesses over
here. We have a portfolio of businesses
here. And I was taking on a new
business. It was too much. I was really
getting close to burnout. I had to sit
inside of a awful PowerPoint for eight
hours in order to realize it. It's
totally normal, but it doesn't have to
be you. The lesson here is every job
sucks. If you want to do what you love,
you have to expect many years of doing
what you hate. The only way to get back
into loving it again is to set yourself
up in the right way. The day someone
steals all your homework. So, at some
point when you are in business, somebody
will take all the ideas that you have
because they're brilliant. They will
take those ideas, they will label them
their own, and they will run away off
into the distance. It's kind of part of
the game. That's why they say the best
form of flattery is mimicry. I'm not
sure I agree with that, but it is what
happened. There was a guy that we were
going to hire, uh, let's call him
Robbie, and he was going to be head of
marketing for one of our companies. And
we get pretty far down the lane with
hiring Robbie. And then, uh, I have an
offer that Robbie and I have agreed on.
And last minute, I think after he
thought that I was super vested in him
as a candidate, he basically increased
his offer to something crazy that made
no sense for the business and what the
role was. And I said, like, Robbie, we
can't do that. we're not going to go
that way. And he said, "Well, then, you
know, I'm not interested." And by that
time, I had shown him like a lot of
stuff behind the scenes. And so,
wouldn't you know, about 30 days later,
dear old Robbie comes out and tries to
compete in the exact same market for
that business. Hats off to Robbie. His
emails look just like ours. His
marketing materials just like ours. In
fact, he tried to steal some of our
employees, and one of the signs that he
had out was very similar to ours. it
didn't end up working. But the amount of
energy and stress that you will feel
when somebody takes your ideas and tries
to run with them and you don't expect
that to happen is huge. Once you realize
that it'll it's going to happen to you
at some way, shape or form. You just go,
I will outwork and I will outsmart
anybody. So, let him try. And thank God
now I look at it and I'm like, Robbie
did me a huge favor because he could
have come in and run my business and
left me in a really difficult spot. He
did the best thing ever. He told me
upfront who he was. He got out of the
way and now we are both out of each
other's lives. Somebody will eventually
steal your homework. The lesson here is
don't waste time convincing people that
you're right and to side with you. Let
them fear going up against you. And I
think Robbie feared that after it didn't
work out for him. A hard but true
lesson. 99% of your problems will never
actually happen. And the 0.5% that do
happen, you're going to survive because
all evidence points to you already
having survived to this day. Now, your
business might not survive in that
moment, but you as a human will because
you have up until now. When you know
what's coming, you can do what the few
do, which is actually prepare. And when
I look at the landscape today, I think
we may be stepping into another 2008
2009. And the market kind of agrees with
me. But even during these massive
downturns and times of difficulty, if we
are the few who do the work up front, we
can be part of the many that actually
profit from it. If you are in the
startup game, give yourself a break.
It's hard out there. Most people are
failing and most people have had this
happen to them at one point or another.
You're not alone. You will fail. That
part is true because entrepreneurship is
hard. You'll get sued, lose money, lose
respect, lose friends, lose time. But
working a 9 to5 is hard. All of it is
hard. So choose your hard. At least now
you can choose your hard with your eyes
wide

## 34. Everything They Teach You At Goldman Sachs

This is Goldman Sachs, where the world's
most powerful governments, corporations,
and billionaires go to figure out what
to do with their money. Almost nobody
gets access to that advice, but I worked
there, and what I learned from the
inside changed how I build wealth
forever. These are the lessons very few
people ever get to learn.
Get a minute, Donna.
Number one, your first 500k is for
losing. So, I say lose somebody else's
money. Like, one of the best pieces of
advice I got at Goldman came from a
mentor I had there, and it's probably
going to piss you off cuz I kind of felt
that way at the time. He said, "Your
first half a million dollars of
investing experience is for losing."
He's like, "You will lose it. You'll buy
at the top. You'll make bets that seem
obvious that are
catastrophically wrong in hindsight. So,
why would you lose your own money? Why
not lose somebody else's first?" What's
cool is when you work at a big
institution, Goldman, JP Morgan,
BlackRock, you make mistakes on their
dime. This is true for you at any
company when you first start out. Like,
the companies don't care as much because
they budget it. It's called a risk
budget. So, they won't fire you over a
learning curve mistake. That's just the
cost of doing business at scale. But, if
you lose money, that might be your rent
or your savings, and that's when panic
decisions, they start to come and try to
claw it back, which makes it worse. The
most dangerous advice circling the
internet right now is just go be an
entrepreneur tomorrow. But,
statistically, it's actually terrible
advice for most people. 90% of startups
fail. So, if your first mistakes are
inevitable, and they are, why not do it
on somebody else's dime? Then go out on
your Goldman made me risk adverse. So,
before I make any major move now, I run
it through what I call the OPX check.
Three questions, you can steal them.
Whose capital can I use instead of mine?
Whose resources can I use instead of
mine? Whose network can I use instead of
mine? If the answer to all three is my
own, I'm probably not thinking about it
right. So, I use this term OPX. It
stands for other people's X, where X is
whatever asset you need, money,
contacts, infrastructure, credibility.
Goldman never takes a position without
limiting their own exposure first. This
is how you do the same thing at any
level. That's rule number one.
>>
>> Number two, this is a hard one. Cut your
bottom 20%. So, every year this place
fires the bottom 20% of its people. And
I used to think that was brutal because
it is. Until I realized every single
high performer in the world does the
exact same thing. They're just quieter
about it. Like I watched a guy, I'll
call him Jack, see himself trending
toward the bottom 20% performance at
this company. And instead of fixing his
numbers, he spent six months kind of
brilliantly engineering the appearance
of better numbers. Could you imagine the
amount of energy and effort spent
looking successful instead of actually
becoming it? Like it's such a waste. So,
Goldman ended up firing him anyway with
a bad recommendation. And a bad
recommendation from this place is
basically like a career bomb. And you
never really walk away from it. So, like
a decade later I ran into Jack. And he
is, you know, not living his best life.
And it wasn't actually that he lacked
talent at all. He was super smart. He
just couldn't face the decision of what
was happening and wasn't willing to do
the hard work instead. So, what can you
take from this? An annual audit of
everything you're carrying. Your
clients, your habits, your circle. Cut
the bottom 20% each year. Every year.
So, the clients costing you energy while
you're barely moving the needle, they're
gone. The morning habits that are
deciding what your years look like, gone
if they don't help. The people in your
circle quietly capping your ceiling,
well, get those out of the way. Because
every high performer has to cut to make
room to grow. Just like the flowers here
have to be pruned every year. The only
question is whether you're honest enough
to actually name what your bottom 20%
is.
The third one, I guess, is be honest
even when you're about to publicly fail.
The most expensive mistake I watched
people make at Goldman was nothing to do
with a bad trade. It was about what they
chose to do the moment they realized
they were falling behind. My colleague
Jack's mistake was really not him
failing, but him lying. You know, I've
seen this pattern everywhere in life.
You know, someone falls short, they know
it, and they start spinning, building
elaborate story around the gap instead
of just like, "Yeah, that is true. Now
I'm going to close the gap." So, you
know, we all manage how it looks instead
of fixing what it is. All that happens
then is you construct a house of cards
and the house falls. People are actually
going to have more respect for you when
you say, "I'm failing at this. Here's
why. Here's what I'm doing about it."
than they ever would have for someone
who gets caught in a lie, because the
truth is you almost always get caught.
Goldman's culture was like intense, and
I wasn't there for very long, but you
knew where you stood, and you had a
choice. Own it and course correct or
become a victim and start trying again.
Literally every person who I've watched
choose to lie instead of course correct,
well, they never came back from it. The
ones who owned their failures, some of
them went on to do huge things, because
people remember they were honest under
pressure. So, as my dad says, tell the
truth even when it's going to cost you.
Number four is what people think about
you is actually your asset. You should
treat it like one. "I don't care what
people think" is one of the most
expensive lies you can tell yourself.
And I actually remember Goldman learning
that the hard way during the the
financial crisis. They weren't the
primary villain of the collapse,
actually. It was like Countrywide and
predatory mortgage originators. They
were writing these terrible loans at
scale, not Goldman. So, why did Goldman
get absolutely destroyed in the court of
public opinion? Because they were really
bad at one thing. They let other people
write their narrative, and they let it
be wrong. And it cost them billions in
reputations, and relationships, and
deals that probably never happened, and
talent, you know, that went elsewhere.
Your version, yeah, maybe it's not
billions at this point, but it's just as
real. If people carry a story about you,
deserved or not, that you're unreliable,
difficult, someone who cuts corners, the
introductions that could have changed
your life, well, they don't even get
made because nobody's going to announce
that they're not going to do something
big for you. So, the opportunity just
goes to somebody else. Your reputation
is an asset, and like any asset, it
either compounds or it's going to decay
depending on how much attention you pay
to it. Think hard about how you show up,
what you say publicly, what story people
tell you the moment you leave the room.
What do people say about you? Because
the story is being written whether
you're involved or not. The only
question is whether you actually wrote
the thing or not.
Lesson number five is stop trying to get
into easy doors. I went through
somewhere between seven and 12
interviews to get into this place,
Goldman. They make it brutally hard on
purpose, but once you understand why, it
changes how you go after any other
opportunity in life. Like, you had to
fly to headquarters in person, prepare a
full presentation and deliver it live,
then sit through the role plays where
partners challenge your assumptions in
real time. Like, you said this was going
to happen. I don't believe you. Back it
up. And you either have an answer or you
didn't. And, you know, back in the day,
I beat out hundreds of candidates for
that seat, and I was just a little
starting role analyst. But Goldman on
your resume is kind of like a skeleton
key. It can get you in just about
anywhere later in life. People even
bring that up about me now despite all
my other accomplishments. Like, the
harder it is to get into something, the
more valuable it is to be inside, and
that's not an accident. So, I see too
many people today trying to get into
easy doors because they get you easy
outcomes. If somebody right now is
trying to apply for a job, if you're
trying to apply for a job, the rooms
that are actually hard to access are
where your trajectory changes. So, go.
Do like 15 interviews for a place. Do a
project. Do hard work. Don't listen to
the people on the internet that tell you
not to do that. Most people do not try
hard enough to get where they are. They
send a bunch of applications, do one
interview, call it that an attempt, and
that is absolute lunacy. You have to
outwork hundreds of people for a single
seat, and I would do it again tomorrow.
I did it back in the day because the
more exclusive that door is, the more
worth it to throw yourself at it
repeatedly without embarrassment until
you get it cuz once you open
they can never take that back.

>> Number six, duration of work beats
output of work, especially when you're
young and building. You know, because
you're behind. When you walk into
Goldman Sachs as a public school kid,
like, you know, me, I realized pretty
quickly I was in a room full of people
who've been preparing for this their
whole lives, and so like, you know, they
knew the vocabulary. They'd summered in
places, and I didn't even know what
summering, you know, was as a verb. They
use shorthand like, um, lezzing, which
means leverage finance. I didn't even
know what leverage finance was. So,
like, rich people build a language that
keeps other people out, whether by
design or not. And you have to learn to
just play. And in order to play, you got
to get fluent if you actually want to
win. I couldn't out-credential or
out-network them. Duration was the only
lever I had, another fancy word for
saying like, can I do this for a really
long time? And so, you know, when I was
starting at Goldman, I was the first one
in and the last one out every day for
most of my first year, at least in our
small little group. And I remember there
was a managing director in our group,
his name was Jim. He was a former
military, super hardcore, and barely
talked to any of us. He was in at 6:30
in the morning. So, I made sure I was
there before him, and I was there when
he left. And eventually he noticed and
started giving me like shot after shot
that nobody else got. And so, that meant
I got more exposure and more
opportunities because he saw I was
willing to work. I was hungry. So, I
watched this play out the same way at my
own company. Now, you know, we just
promoted someone who is not the most
credentialed person in the building, but
he's always in earlier and out later
than everyone else and building cool,
weird side projects. It's not like he's
just wasting his time. And he makes more
money than people who've been here
longer. Because when you're young and
behind, duration is probably your only
lever. Like, you're not smarter than
other people, perhaps. You're not more
experienced, but you can work harder.
And so, the question is, that door,
maybe it's not open right now, but it
will be when you do the things you have
to to get noticed. That's what I found.
This last meeting really reminds me of
number seven. Do not brown nose mediocre
people. It just makes you mediocre. I
mean, you want to pick the right person
to impress. And that is a skill that can
change your life. Yet, most people are
terrible at it. Like, when I was at
Goldman, we had multiple managers and
the contrast between two of them made
this lesson like impossible for me to
miss. So, Jim, who I mentioned earlier,
was serious, respected. And then there
was, you know, this other guy. I'll be
kind. And I won't say his name. But I
remember, you can just picture this. He
once opened a team meeting by showing us
a video of himself as a kid kickboxing
in a ring. Like, he had Rocky music
playing and I think he was trying to
inspire us like during the financial
crisis. I'm not really sure. I mean, I'm
sitting there thinking, what is
happening right now? And the way he
walked was like Weekend at Bernie's
every other day, but now I'm being mean.
But like, he is completely unserious in
a place that rewards nothing but
seriousness. Now, one woman on our team
figured out he was easy to work. Like,
she brought him a birthday cake on his
birthday and the two of them weirdly ate
it alone in his office. Like, super
weird energy. However, like, climbed up
within our relative group pretty quickly
under him. But none of the real leaders
at Goldman took him or her seriously and
her path went nowhere because of that.
So, like, the real players there had
zero interest in being flattered. And
that is true everywhere. You cannot
charm your way into their sphere. And
the tell is really this. If you can BS
your way into somebody's good graces,
they're not worth being your champion.
What it takes to get into the winner's
sphere is exactly what makes them worth
being in. Like, if you're awesome and
exceptional, the real players, they come
to you. And that's the whole point of
the game. So, don't feel like you have
to brown-nose people that you don't even
want to shake hands with. You know what
I'm saying?
You know,
I think you got to make yourself so
valuable that you write your own terms.
So, that's number eight. A colleague at
Goldman actually taught me so much about
how to create freedom for yourself. Her

And that's just what it cost. But, if

you come or go. You do what you want.
You come in when you want. You leave
when you want because output when you're
a winner is the only metric. So, get
that right. Freedom comes after it. I've
seen that time and again.
Number nine, actually, the partnership
model is the real wealth vehicle. And
our
entire generation is asleep on it. So,
like, this company, Goldman, has been
making its partners extraordinarily rich
for 150 years. And almost none of it
came from an IPO or one huge exit. The
most profitable law firms in the world
run the same way. So does like private
equity, top insurance companies,
investment managers, Wall Street
broadly. They're all partnerships. And
like stick with me here for a while.
Like startup culture sold our entire
generation on equity. Get in early, you
know, get the equity, pray for the exit,
and maybe you'll get rich. But most
people actually don't. So the math on
startup equity is bad for most people
who play it. And I've done the opposite
my whole career. Like every major
business I've built has been a
partnership. And you can make
extraordinary money in them even as
employees. The Goldman partners I
watched, they didn't get rich from
salary alone. They had bonuses and
decades of personal investments all
compounding at once. I know a guy at
BlackRock who makes $500 million in a
single year. And that comes from
compounding partnership economics over
time and doing deals. So when you
optimize for upside tied to performance,
not equity in somebody else's company,
you can make a lot more. So I like to
ask myself this question. You can steal
it. If I do X, I make more, and that's
the structure you want. And where the
actual upside comes from when you're
starting out is risking a little bit and
betting on yourself. At least that's
what I think.

>> Number 10. Start investing in off-market

to make generational wealth in decades
yet the thing is very few people are
seeing it you're going to be one of them
I went on the biggest podcasts and

## 37. Forget MBAs, THIS is The Fastest Path to Wealth

This building behind me costs students
and taxpayers hundreds of
millions of dollars. But what if I told
you the entire institution might be
built on a lie? Because if education is
supposed to be an investment, MBAs are
starting to look like a scam. You're
paying 200,000 to learn outdated
frameworks from professors who've never
run a business. What if the smartest
thing was skipping school altogether and
doing this instead? By the way, I'm Cody
Sanchez, founder of Contrarian
Thinking, and we've built dozens of
businesses with mostly non MBAs. So
remember throughout this what we're
getting into in this video anyone
can do. It's not about the money
you have. It's not the education you
have. It's something else entirely.
Let's get into it.
See, I spent the last four years
teaching other people to buy and build
businesses. I worked on Wall Street for
a decade before we built out our holding
company, investing everything from
robots to roofers. And I also made a
bunch of videos on the internet. Or as
my grandma says, that's a job. So today,
I'm going to start us with a powerful
idea. Education is investment in your
future. But if traditional business
education were a stock, it would be the
worst performing investment of the next
20 years. If you want to go study
business, where should you go then? It's
a rhetorical question kind of because of
course you'd go to business school,
right? Except today I'm going to show
you that is no longer an obvious choice
at all. Here's a recent article from the
New York Times. Since you guys don't
have bionic vision, I'm just
going to tell you what it says. It
describes how businesses are complaining
about MBA graduates. What frustrates
them? You guys have probably
heard this before. over reliance on
mathematical management, expectations of
becoming a CEO in four weeks. It
also warns that at a time when American
business is changing, business schools
suffer from cookie cutter syndrome. They
offer insufficient training for
entrepreneurship and small business. My
guess is some of you are sitting there
saying, "Yeah, that sounds about right."
Except I lied to you. This study isn't
recent at all. It's from January 1981 or
almost 50 years ago when the starting
salary of a Stanford grad was $32,000.
Now, let me be clear. I got an
MBA. It opened doors for me. And the top
schools know their customers. Like, what
are us millennials more afraid of than
war and death? FOMO. They're afraid of
missing out. And what do the top schools
really do well? They foster elite
networks or the idea of them. So
MBAs will tell you the network alone.
It's worth the cost. And yeah, you could
say maybe that's true cuz look, the S&P
500, arguably one of the best wealth
creation tools in history, is like
largely a network of MBAs. But bottom
line, NBA programs, while they cranked
out plenty of talent, today they're
facing what I think is their first real
crisis of purpose since creation. Two
reasons. One, they're just not getting
people jobs like they used to. Which
explains this, why these poor guys who
took the traditional elite MBA path, get
a degree, get a role in finance, big
tech, or consulting, aka some of the
industries most immediately impacted by
AIdriven disruption, cost cutting, are
now asking why. You know the old saying,
nobody ever got fired for hiring
McKenzie. New saying, "Good chances are
you'll get fired by working at
McKenzie."
>> Good luck with your layoffs. All right.
I hope your firings go really well.
>> Excellent.
>> Great.
>> And then people will say, "Well, what
about MBAs for entrepreneurship?" Well,
one of the most successful business
builders in history. Sure, many of them
have MBAs, but go to a party in Silicon

## 58. How This Garbage Man Built A Billion Dollar Empire

How does a man go from hauling garbage
in a beat up truck to becoming one of
the richest self-made billionaires in
American history? Because that's exactly
what Wayne Huizenga did. We've studied
him for years. Today we break down how
he pulled it off step by step. Wild
part? He used a framework, one that you
can apply, too, to go from where you are
right now to where you actually want to
be, rich, free, and turning the smell of
garbage into millions.
February 14th, 1962. It's a humid night
in South Florida. While most of the city
sleeps, 25-year-old Wayne Huizenga fires
up the engine of a battered garbage
truck. The air is thick with the sweet
and sour smell of trash and diesel
gasoline. Wayne grips the steering
wheel, his knuckles covered in filth.
But this is no ordinary night for him.
It's the very first route of his own
garbage business.
He doesn't know it yet, but this lone
truck rolling in the darkness is the
monumental start of his empire built on
trash.
But Wayne's story isn't just about
trash. It's a master class in leverage
you can apply to your life. The kind of
leverage that takes you from pushing a
garbage can to owning three pro sports
teams. The leverage ladder. Level one,
sweat leverage. Wayne is no stranger to
working hard. He grew up in Chicago,
raised by a strict blue-collar father
who ran a small construction company.
The world of physical labor was in his
blood. From a young age, his father
drilled the truth into him, "You'll
never make real money working for
somebody else."
But Wayne didn't actually listen at
first. He wasn't very good at college,
he dropped out, he joined the army, and
he came back ready to build something of
his own, but he wasn't very successful
at all. In fact, he failed and struggled
again and again. Then in 1962, he
convinces a local hauler to sell him one
used truck and 20 commercial accounts
with 500 bucks. That was it. His days
began at 3:00 a.m. hauling trash. By
noon, he'd scrub the grime off, change
into a suit, and hit the streets.
Morning, garbage man. Afternoon,
salesman. Door-to-door, one shopkeeper
at a time. That's sweat leverage. He was
trading his energy and time for income.
You see, he was at level one of the
leverage ladder, but it didn't stay that
way. And what happened next is where we
all want to go.
Level two, skill leverage.
Wayne wasn't just hustling. He was
absorbing, watching, experimenting. He
studied his customers. What made them
switch providers? What pissed them off?
How to keep them happy? Rich people
wanted to pay more for premium service.
So, he made a mental playbook. Show up
early. Never miss a day. Under promise,
over deliver. Always smile. Have
expensive options. Every satisfied
business owner meant five more
introductions. He learned what types of
businesses were easiest to convert, what
objections were smoke versus fire, where
his competition got lazy. He was willing
to push. Within six years, his company,
Southern Sanitation Service, grew from a
guy in a truck to 20 trucks covering two
counties. Wayne wasn't scaling himself
anymore. He was scaling knowledge,
sales, ops, service, leadership. Because
once you master skill leverage, you
unlock the rungs above it. Level three,
systems leverage. Build once, sell
continuously. You build repeatable
processes that earn even when you're
sleeping. In 1968, Wayne's
cousin-in-law, Dean, flew down from
Chicago with a bold proposition. "Let's
merge. Let's build the first national
garbage company." That meant Wayne gave
up control. It meant he had to handle
unions, boss guys, mafia, regulations,
cutthroat competition. It was risky, but
Wayne saw it for what it was, a chance
to scale beyond the borders of his
county and into a nationwide empire. He
wasn't just hauling garbage. He was
designing a system. So, they renamed it
Waste Management, Inc. And from day one,
their only plan for growth was buy more
things with other people's money. Wayne
merged with Dean Buntrock's Chicago
operations, but he didn't just merge the
routes. He merged landfill assets
because Dean owned a bunch of them. And
by bringing that into the fold, Wayne
and Dean created a network advantage.
Local haulers now had to pay them to
dump there. Their competitors paid them.
They went across the US and tried to
find small, beat-up family businesses
and buy them using seller financing. A
company in Ohio figured out a more
efficient routing system. Oh, they just
stole that homework and applied it to
their entire business. Their hauler in
Texas had a better maintenance cycle.
Perfect. They rolled it out across the
company. By 1972, just 4 years after the
merger, they had acquired 90 companies.
That is one company every 3 days from a
guy that had no money and never raised a
ton of capital to do this. Wayne also
invested in technology before it was
cool. Everybody thought he was nuts. Why
are you bringing all these computers?
You're a garbage man. But, he brought in
reduced billionaires. He brought in
software to manage accounts. He actually
didn't think about himself as a garbage
man at all. He said that garbage
collection was just a company that
scaled. So, how do I do something that
becomes a replication engine?
Because once you build a system that
works without you, the next step is
finding who else can run it. Level four,
capital leverage. You start using other
people's money to grow faster. This is
where Wayne flips the game board. Up
until now, he's been buying companies in
the old-fashioned way, using profits and
whatever financing he could scrape
together. But, that approach has limits.
You run out of cash, you grow slow, you
start playing defense. Wayne wasn't
interested in building a good business.
He was playing to win nationally. So, he
turned to capital leverage, the art of
using other people's money to go faster,
bigger, and further than your bank
account allows. Here's how it started.
Instead of paying cash for every
acquisition, Wayne started offering
stock. A simple shift, but a powerful
one. In fact, after Waste Management
went public, Wayne used shares, not
cash, to buy 133 small haulers in just
10 months across the Sunbelt,
integrating their routes and their
disposal assets to make him impossible
to compete with. Every time a competitor
got too good, he bought them.
Because stock doesn't drain your cash
reserves. It lets you buy businesses
without debt. It's literally just a
piece of paper. But it creates shared
upside, so everyone is rowing in the
same direction. And the sellers loved
it. Why take a lump sum when you can own
a piece of a rocket ship?
So, this was emotional leverage. Wayne
wasn't asking people to sell, he was
inviting them to level up.
Then came the 1971 IPO. Wayne stands on
the floor of the New York Stock Exchange
wearing a suit, watching the first
shares of Waste Management trade hands.
The company raised $3 million, around
$31 million today, but more importantly,
that was the first millions that they'd
raised. And it gave them access to
institutional capital, credit lines,
underwriters, and a public currency that
could buy entire companies.
Now, they went on a buying spree. They
didn't just grow in size, they grew in
valuation. They were using capital to
acquire revenue, which made the stock
more valuable, which let them acquire
even more companies. That's compound
leverage in motion. And this playbook
didn't stop with trash. Wayne would go
on to use the same capital strategy to
build Blockbuster Video, AutoNation, and
other multi-billion dollar companies. He
understood that money isn't the finish
line, it's a force multiplier. And once
you can move capital at scale, the next
step is finding the people who can carry
the vision even further.
He said, "Some people dream of success,
while other people get up every morning
and make it happen."
Level five people leverage. Wayne
Huizenga knew something most
entrepreneurs learn too late. You can't
scale an empire alone. He was never
afraid to hire people smarter than him,
more experienced than him, more
specialized. In fact, he sought them
out. When Waste Management acquired a
new company, Wayne didn't wipe the slate
clean. He usually kept the original
owner on board. Why? Because these folks
knew their markets better than anyone.
They had relationships, network,
knowledge, and a pride in owning the
thing. But most importantly, they were
shareholders.
Wayne wasn't turning them into
employees. He was turning them into
partners. This was people leverage in
action. Retain proven operators, align
incentives with equity, free up your own
time to focus on strategy. Wayne created
regional leadership layers. He didn't
have to micromanage. And that system's
decision-making then meant that those
people could run the systems. And look,
that type of trust doesn't come easy.
Wayne later admitted in an interview
that when Waste Management started
scaling, he didn't actually know how to
manage these people. He's like, "I had
to learn how to deal with people over
the telephone many miles apart." But
after doing it a bunch, you finally
figure out how to hire the right people
and make things happen. That's what
people leverage actually looks like.
It's not some magical just hire great
people shortcut. It's awkward phone
calls, missed targets. It's learning to
let go when you want to do it yourself.
But once he learned high-level operators
exist, that's when the whole game
changed.
Blockbuster, the second empire.
In 1987, Wayne was semi-retired and
bored. So naturally, he bought a video
rental chain with 19 locations for
pennies on the dollar. He applied the
exact same people first model he used in
garbage. He looked for operators who
could run stores, manage regions, and
scale systems. In less than three years,
Blockbuster went from 19 stores to over
1,600.
He understood something most tech
founders don't. Video rentals were
hyper-local, operationally complex, and
replicable if by the right people.
So in '94 he sold Blockbuster to Viacom
for 8.4 billion dollars. But he wasn't
done.
AutoNation, the third empire.
Everyone said car sales couldn't be
scaled. It's too fragmented, they said,
too old school. Wayne looked exactly for
that. He bought a few dealerships, then
a few more, then he put in systems, then
he put in people, then he put
incentivized performance with ownership.
Before long, AutoNation became the
largest car retailer in the United
States with over 20 billion in annual
revenue. He said, "We're looking for
something where we can make something
happen in industry where the competition
is asleep, hasn't taken advantage." Not
because Wayne would micromanage
everyone, but because he knew people
plus structure wins. He didn't build
companies, he built leadership
pipelines. And that's why people
leverage is the turning point on the
leverage ladder because it allows you to
scale just about anything. This is what
separates the builders from the owners
and the owners from the legends. Level
six, attention leverage. The world now
watches what you do. Wayne Huizenga was
never chasing fame. He wasn't loud, he
wasn't flashy. He didn't slap his name
on skyscrapers. In fact, he wore an old
school watch, he hated buying new
clothes, his jeans were always ripped,
and he had one favorite pair of boots.
But by the late '90s, attention found
him anyway. Why? Because attention is a
natural byproduct of impact. At this
point, Wayne had built three
billion-dollar companies. He had taken
multiple businesses public. He also
owned three major sports teams, the
Miami Dolphins, Florida Marlins, Florida
Panthers. He even danced to the Hokey
Pokey in view of the crowd at a Dolphins
game. Ludicrous, yes, but it made a
billionaire relatable. He wasn't just a
shadow man in a suit behind the scenes.
The crowd cheered, reporters snapped
photos, the moment made headlines. It
showed Wayne that he was part of the
story and he didn't take himself too
seriously. And after that, well, the
journalists came. They wanted
interviews, forecasts, attention. He
wanted to reverse engineer him, but they
wanted to make him matter.
And all of a sudden,
his moves did matter. And that's the
hidden power of attention leverage. You
don't actually have to shout to be seen.
You just have to do something remarkable
enough long enough that people start
listening. What's interesting to me here
is Wayne never invented anything. He
didn't have a startup ever.
He bought businesses that were old and
then made them viral and interesting and
huge with really four things: acquiring,
systematizing, compounding, hiring over
and over and over again. So, what's the
real takeaway? Wayne Huizenga didn't win
because he was the smartest or the most
innovative. In fact, he wasn't either of
those things. He didn't have a fancy
degree, he didn't know how to code, he
didn't raise venture capital, he didn't
come from millions, he didn't go to the
best schools out there. He just climbed
the leverage ladder. Sweat to skill,
skill to systems, systems to capital,
capital to people, people to attention.
That's the same framework I use now
decades later when I buy businesses. You
want generational wealth? Don't climb
the corporate ladder, climb the leverage
ladder.
The final ride, 1984. Wayne stepped down
as CEO of Waste Management. To celebrate
his last day, he put on his old
coveralls, hopped into a beat-up garbage
truck, and drove one last truck route.
Even after becoming worth hundreds of
millions of dollars, he wanted to
remember where it all began, as a
garbage man. That's [ __ ] cool.
If this inspired you, even just a
little, maybe it's time for you to make
your move. If you're not already
subscribed, do it. Share this with a
friend. I'll show you how to buy boring
businesses, build leverage, and maybe
one day
have your own damn garbage truck empire
or something cleaner.

## 61. How To Escape The Rat Race (The Money Game That School Never Taught You)

This is how most people play the game of
money. Sacrificing pawns, obsessing on
the small moves. But real wealth, it's
not about the pawns, it's about moving
pieces that actually change the game.
Because while most people spend their
lives reacting, the wealthier playing to
win. And the worst part, nobody ever
taught you the real rules. Do you all
stay in the job that you pick for the
rest of your life?
>> We are one of the wealthiest nations in
history, and yet seemingly no one has
any money. Which raises a haunting
question. How can you make money and not
lose? In 2016, a lottery winner named
David Lee Edwards, who walked away with
$27 million in winnings, was found
living in a storage unit, penniless,
battling addiction. He had gone from
instant multi-millionaire to broke in
less than a decade. And he is not alone.
Six-figure earners are living paycheck
to paycheck. Celebrities file bankruptcy
after careers worth hundreds of
millions. People are financing their
DoorDash orders. This is not just a
story of bad luck, or greed, or poor
judgment. It's about a system. A game. A
game of money that has rules most people
were never taught. Rules that quietly
separate those who play to survive from
those who play to win. And unless you
learn the rules, you'll spend the rest
of your life running harder and harder
in a race you were never designed to
finish. You see, schools and the systems
are basically prisons. They keep kids
locked up physically and mentally, and
prepare them for jobs that are basically
prisons until they retire and go to a
nursing home, their final prison. So, in
this video, let's pull away the
blindfold. I'm definitely not in the rat
race now, but I used to be. I worked in
corporate jobs in cubicles with padded
walls at Goldman and State Street. I had
a job where I had to clock in and clock
out every time I went to the bathroom at
Vanguard. The bathroom. I refuse to go
back there. It actually scares the hell
out of me. But we're going to expose why
you were never taught the real game of
money, and how the system is structured,
and what you can do today to stop
playing as a pawn on the board, and
start becoming the one making the rules.
Part one, the illusion of the rat race.
Most people's financial lives look like
this. Go to school, get a job, earn
wages, spend them on expenses, repeat.
The cycle is so deeply ingrained that we
don't even question it, but it's a
treadmill, a rat race. Every raise is
matched by a new expense. Every windfall
canceled by a new debt. The faster you
run, the more exhausted you get, and you
never actually move forward. A survey
found that 78% of American workers live
paycheck to paycheck. But what is even
more shocking? Even among people earning
over $100,000 a year, one in 10 are
still broke at the end of every month.
The answer lies in what I call the
consumption trap.
This is what stops you from becoming
rich. This is you. You earn money, you
start motoring, but every time you get
it, you spend it. So, you're forever
stuck in a cycle that never has an end.
Ask 10 people to define money, and
you'll hear some version of this. It's a
medium of exchange. But that's not very
useful. A better way to think of it is,
money is stored value. You exchange
money for something because you believe
its value is equal to the dollars you
gave up. But here's the problem. From an
early age, we are conditioned to see
money not as stored value, but as fuel
for consumption. Good grades, good job,
paycheck, expenses. Entire economies are
built on this cycle. Advertisers fuel
it, credit cards lubricate it, social
media amplifies it. And it creates a
culture where our worth is measured by
what we consume. The car we drive, the
shoes we wear, the vacations we post
online. Dad, why do we have to go to
school? So you can get a job. But why do
I need a job? So you can earn money. But
why do I need money? To buy the stuff
the TV makes us want. But why do I need
to buy stuff? Because if you didn't buy
stuff, everyone would be out of a job,
and no one would have any money. Wait,
so I have to work for the rest of my
life to pay for stuff that I don't want,
just so everyone else can suffer the
same horrible fate as me? Consumption
never creates wealth. At best, managing
your consumption keeps you from falling
apart. At worst, it chains you to debt,
anxiety, and financial fragility. That's
why high earners aren't even safe. 62%
of people making over $300,000 a year
still struggle with credit card debt.
And why millennials now fear death more
than death itself. 66% of us say we're
more scared of running out of money than
dying. Because in the consumption trap,
your entire financial life is defined by
what goes out. And unless you break out
of that paradigm, you'll never have
anything left over to build with.
Part three, defense wins games, but not
championships. Let me be clear.
Mastering consumption, necessary. But
remember, the master was once a
beginner, too. So, you start like a
snowball down the hill. You face your
numbers, you journal expenses, you build
a budget, you live below your means. Out
of its total weekly income, Jack's
family allows so much for food,
household, clothing. Each part of their
regular expenses is allowed for in the
budget. Create an emergency fund, three
to six months of expenses. That's the
minimum. And it's simple, maybe not
easy. A proven savings framework for
every paycheck, 50-30-20. The rule is,
you give 50% of take-home pay to
essentials, only things you need. 30% to
discretionary spending, wants, and 20%
directly to savings. So, you're spending
most of what you earn on your housing,
food, utilities. You're spending 30% on
entertainment, dining out, hobbies. 20%
you pay yourself first. You know why?
Because what should really scare you,
and what scares me, is having to go work
a normal job. A job I hate, where you
take the subway every single day, and a
commute that feels like you're wasting
time to a place where you like nobody
that you work alongside, doing things
that don't stimulate you or inspire you
for a bunch of people where the mission
is not yours. I think that should scare
you, too. So, this is financial defense.
It keeps you in the game, but here's the
paradox. Defense doesn't win
championships. So, yes, cutting
Starbucks might save you $5 a day, but
nobody builds generational wealth by
skipping lattes. You can cut until
you're blue in the face, and the best

Which brings us to the other side of the
equation. Part four, the power of
production. When it comes to wealth,
offense wins. If you think the life I'm
chasing is crazy, one of freedom and
possibility, I think the life most
people accept is crazy, one of monotony
and boredom. Warren Buffett didn't

a billionaire by skipping brunch. They
both mastered their burn, yeah, but the
real wealth came from production.
Production means creating value and
capturing part of it. It looks like
identifying a problem, creating a
solution, delivering that solution at
scale. It's often called the cash flow
quadrant by Robert Kiyosaki, which
categorizes four primary ways people
earn income, employee, self-employed,
business owner, investor. The first two,
you work for other people. The second
two, the money works for you. That's it.
It sounds simple, but it's the one thing
school never taught you. Because schools
weren't designed to teach you to build
wealth, they were designed to produce
workers. Dependable, disciplined,
compliant workers. It's why only 6% of
Americans run a business profitably.
It's why we were taught how to write
resumes, but not balance sheets. Why we
learned quadratic equations, but not
compounding interest. Because if
everyone knew how to play the money
game, who would clock in?
Part five, the hidden rules of the game.
So, what are the real rules? The ones
the wealthy quietly exploit. They can be
boiled down to five concepts.
Compounding, the most powerful force in
finance. Your money earns money, which
earns money, and so on. Time is your
multiplier. Start early, the game bends
to your will. Leverage, using tools,
capital, technology, media, or people to
multiply your efforts. While you trade
hours for wages, the wealthy trade
systems for outcomes. Ownership, the
rich don't just work for businesses,
they own them. Equity, not wages, that's
where wealth accumulates. Taxes, the
wealthy don't just earn differently,
they're taxed differently. Workers pay
the highest rates, owners, investors,
and asset holders use the system itself
as a shield. Income streams, the average
millionaire has seven streams of them.
Capital gains, buying and selling stocks
at a profit. Interest income, which is
lending money. Earned income, working a
job. Profit income, flipping. Rental
income, renting a property. Dividends,
buying dividend paying stocks. And
royalties. These are your rails to
income. These rules aren't secret,
they're just not taught. And because
they're not taught, most people don't
play them. Many people ask me, Cody,
nine out of 10 businesses fail, why
would you even start? Because do you
have another choice? If you don't play,
you end up back in the race. Remember
the average person lives 78.4 years.
That's 78 summers, 78 falls, 78
birthdays. That's it. Scares me to think
about wasting even one.
Part six, from player to architect. So,
how do you stop being a pawn on the
board and start becoming the one who
makes the rules?
It starts with a mindset shift. Stop
seeing your income as a paycheck. Start
seeing it as fuel for freedom. If you
earn wages, great. That's your seed
capital. Use it, too, escape the
consumption trap, build your emergency
fund, buy yourself time to think and
plan. Channel the surplus into
production, invest in assets, in
businesses, in ownership, even if it's
just a slice. Because in the long run,
ownership is the only escape from the
race. The rat race doesn't end by
running faster, it ends by changing the
game you're playing. By realizing that
action is the fundamental key to all
success. Part seven, your next move.
Here's your road map.
Audit your relationship with money.
Write down every single dollar in and
out. Shine light on the numbers you've
been avoiding. That's how you stabilize,
build your budget, create that fund, get
out of high interest debt. Once you
shift focus, you can have stable
instead of obsessing over only cutting.
You start thinking about creating. Then
you move to producing and owning.
Whether through a side hustle, a small
business, or equity in your job, find a
way to own a piece of the value you
create. Then you learn the rules. Study
compounding, learn leverage, understand
taxes. The earlier you master these, the
faster the game tilts in your favor. And
remember, God gave you this mindset for
a reason.
And you know this truth, the time is
always now. Because money is not evil,
it's not good, it's simply a mirror, a
reflection of the choices we make. The
same $100 can buy drugs or fund your
kids future. The same paycheck can be
consumed on liabilities or invested into
assets that compound. Same tool,
different outcome. Most people spend
their lives as players in a game they
never designed, but you don't have to.
Because once you understand the hidden
rules, once you realize the system was
never built to make you free, you can
start building your own system. And when
you do, you stop being a pawn and start
becoming the architect.

## 63. How To Grow Your Business SO Fast It Feels Illegal

Every guru online is telling you the
exact same thing. Run more ads, hook
harder, post three times a day, build
the funnel, give away a free PDF, sell
cheap or sell expensive, just pick one.
I've heard it all. And I am here to tell
you today that almost none of that is
how I grew them. The real problem is
something else entirely. Everyone
teaching business growth learned it from
building one business, usually a
personal brand, usually info products.
Those are the people on the internet,
and usually they sell ads. I learned it
from buying and operating dozens of
businesses across all industries. That's
a totally different school. There are
six ways to grow a business fast. Most
people know one. I'm going to give you
all six, and I'm going to tell you
something that most educators won't. The
fastest one has nothing to do
with marketing.
Lever one, what I call the premium flip.
You're going to flip from competing on
price to competing on value.
Hard truth, you have likely been
undercharging for years. I can say that
without knowing anything about your
business. I mean, I've looked at
thousands and thousands of businesses,
and as an investor, the problem is
almost never prices set too high, it's
that you don't value yourself enough.
So, I bought an online education
business that was selling its content
for 29 bucks a month. The owner hadn't
raised prices in 3 years,
convinced himself that the market
wouldn't bear it, all of his clients
were too cheap. First thing I did was
just test that. I raised the price, not
from 29, but to 299 bucks a month, 10
times what it had been. Do you know how
many of our customers we lost, you guys?
We lost 3% of our customers. Annual
revenue went way up. So, the owner had
just been leaving money on the table for
years, not because the market wouldn't
pay, because he never asked. By the way,
don't forget, on a million-dollar
business, 20% increase in prices is
$200,000 in new revenue without you
having to find a single customer. You
spent 3 years trying to grow from 800k
to a million, you could have done it in
30 days. What's crazy is there's a bunch
of studies, like this one, that show 55%
of companies surveyed by Bain were able
to raise list prices and make more
money. What to do? Here's how to know if
you can raise prices. Won't I lose more
customers if I raise prices, Cody? I
know lots of people ask this. The truth
is, price-sensitive customers are often
what? Tell me in the comments. The
worst. They jump ship the second a
cheaper option comes along. By raising
prices, you have to actually filter all
those people out. You find your fans.
Then they'll say, tell me if this is you
thinking in your head, what if my
competitors undercut me? Your prices
shouldn't be your selling point. If
somebody wants to buy something cheaper,
you just go, yeah, you could buy it
cheaper, but cheaper doesn't mean
better. When you're the best at what you
do, price becomes the least important
thing. So, remember, your aim in
business is to get some sort of a
monopoly, right? That's the ability to
set your own prices. Okay, what do I
mean by this? I mean what you're looking
for in your business is the ability for
you to create unique value. Here's how
you can tell.
Are you selling out? You can't keep
inventory on shelves. When you're
drowning in orders, it's a clear signal
the market wants to pay you more for
your offer. So, raise your prices.
Second, if you haven't raised your
prices in 1 year or more, then it's
about time to raise your prices. 2 to 5%
annual bump at least. Your costs have
certainly increased, so you're going to
lose money by not increasing your
prices. The third is, what are your
competitors doing? Are they charging
more than you? More often than not, they
are. So, price can be an indicator of
value. Don't let low prices actually
paint a wrong picture that you're cheap
because you should be. I mean, think
about it for a second. Like, imagine
that super fancy Louis Vuitton bag. What
if it cost 30 bucks? High-end brands are
not scared about their prices because it
filters out penny pinchers and signals
value. Let me tell you right now how to
double the amount of revenue you have in
your business if you don't have these.
Number one, do you just sell things one
time or do you have a subscription? If
you don't have a subscription, add it.
Number two, do you have pricing tiers?
Low, medium, high? Most people choose
the medium, so make sure that that is

Finally, upsells. If they buy your

if they can do a little add-on like

counter when you get there that will
increase what's called your LTV, your

So, raise your prices 10 to 30% on your

online sales. Most people confuse

## 65. How To Speak Like The Richest 1%

When you speak, you are being judged. So
if you want to be respected, you need to
be in complete control of this and this
and this. So today, I'm going to show
you real proof from business leaders,
experts, celebrities, and myself
after running a portfolio of dozens of
businesses to command respect and
detention. Because if you can't
take control of any room when you walk
in, how can you expect to take control
of your income, your opportunities, and
your future? By the way, if you don't
know me, I'm Cody Sanchez and I run a
ninef figureure holding company called
Contrarian Thinking. Let's get you in
the CEO's seat. Focusing on words.
You know what separates people
who command respect from those who
don't? It's not their title, their bank
account, or even their expertise. It's
actually how they speak. And I'm not
talking about having a fancy vocabulary
or perfect grammar. I'm talking about
simple shifts and how you use your voice
that will make people lean in, listen
up, and take you seriously. Let
me show you this clip from Jordan
Peterson so we can steal his homework.
>> Let's say you've got a kid and you want
the kid to improve. You don't set them a
bar that's so high that it's impossible
for them to attain it. You take a look
at the kid and you think, "Okay, this
kid's got this range of skill. Here's a
challenge we can throw at him or her
that exceeds their current level of
skill, but gives them a reasonable
probability of success." Do you notice a
few things about it? His pace he's
speaking at. He doesn't rush his words
like he's apologizing. He speaks
deliberately, like every word matters.
In short, he slows way down.
When we're defending an unpopular
opinion, like he often does, or if we're
trying to prove our worth, most of us
speed up like we're afraid someone's
going to cut us off. But here's the
thing, rushing makes you sound anxious,
not authoritative.
When you slow down, you give yourself
space to think, which means fewer ums
and more confidence. A perfect place to
slow down is in meetings. I
remember explicitly the first time I had
a big chance to talk to the CEOs at a
company called Vanguard back in the day.
I was probably 21 years old.
When they said, "Do you have any
questions?" My hand shot up so fast. The
